agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 <100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot path (feature lookup + model inference) from the cold path (training pipeline + model registry) to independently optimize latency and throughput. A lightweight feature store bridges both paths. Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a blue/green model deployment pattern behind a routing layer. Designed for a 4-person team — simplicity is the governing constraint.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Prediction Service","responsibility":"Serves real-time delivery time predictions. Loads the active model into memory, fetches pre-computed features, runs inference, returns result. Stateless horizontally-scalable service.","boundaries":"Owns only inference. Does not train, evaluate, or manage models. Reads features but never writes them."},{"name":"Feature Store","responsibility":"Two-tier feature storage: (1) Offline store (e.g., Parquet/S3) for training data assembly, (2) Online store (Redis) for low-latency feature serving at prediction time. Computes and materializes features on a schedule and on event ingestion.","boundaries":"Owns feature computation logic and materialization. Does not own raw event storage or model logic."},{"name":"Feature Pipeline","responsibility":"Batch and near-real-time jobs that transform raw delivery/order/weather/courier data into features and write them to both offline and online stores. Runs on a schedule and reacts to new data landing.","boundaries":"Transforms raw data into features. Does not perform training or inference."},{"name":"Training Pipeline","responsibility":"Weekly orchestrated pipeline: assembles training dataset from offline feature store, trains model, evaluates against holdout set and shadow baseline, registers candidate model in Model Registry.","boundaries":"Owns training, hyperparameter tuning, and offline evaluation. Does not deploy models — only registers candidates."},{"name":"Model Registry","responsibility":"Versioned storage of trained model artifacts with metadata (metrics, training data hash, timestamp). Supports tagging (candidate, active, previous). Implemented as S3 + a metadata table (DynamoDB or Postgres).","boundaries":"Owns model artifact lifecycle. Does not decide which model to serve — that's the Deployment Controller's job."},{"name":"Deployment Controller","responsibility":"Promotes candidate models to active after validation gates pass. Manages blue/green model slots in the Prediction Service. Executes rollback by swapping the active pointer back to the previous version. Exposes a CLI/API for manual rollback.","boundaries":"Owns promotion and rollback logic. Does not train or evaluate models."},{"name":"Monitoring & Alerting","responsibility":"Tracks prediction latency (P50/P95/P99), prediction distribution drift, actual-vs-predicted error rate (once delivery completes), model version in use, feature freshness.","boundaries":"Observes all components. Triggers alerts but does not auto-rollback (rollback is manual via Deployment Controller to keep blast radius human-controlled with a 4-person team)."},{"name":"Data Ingestion Layer","responsibility":"Receives raw delivery events (order placed, courier assigned, delivery completed), weather API pulls, and courier load snapshots. Lands data in a raw data lake (S3).","boundaries":"Owns raw data collection and landing. Does not transform or serve data."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Checkout Service (external)","to":"Prediction Service","contract":"POST /predict { order_weight, distance_km, zone_id, request_time_utc } → { estimated_minutes, confidence_interval, model_version } — P99 <100ms SLA","coupling_type":"synchronous REST/gRPC"},{"from":"Prediction Service","to":"Feature Store (Online)","contract":"GET features by (zone_id, time_bucket, courier_region) → { weather_code, avg_courier_load, zone_avg_delivery_min } — P99 <10ms from Redis","coupling_type":"synchronous lookup"},{"from":"Feature Pipeline","to":"Feature Store (Online + Offline)","contract":"WRITE materialized feature vectors keyed by entity + time","coupling_type":"batch/async write"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Feature Store (Offline)","contract":"READ training dataset as assembled feature matrix + labels (actual delivery times)","coupling_type":"batch read"},{"from":"Training Pipeline","to":"Model Registry","contract":"PUT model artifact + metadata (metrics, data version hash, timestamp)","coupling_type":"async write"},{"from":"Deployment Controller","to":"Model Registry","contract":"READ candidate model artifact; UPDATE tags (active/previous)","coupling_type":"synchronous API"},{"from":"Deployment Controller","to":"Prediction Service","contract":"Signal to hot-reload model artifact from registry (e.g., update config, rolling restart, or in-process model swap endpoint)","coupling_type":"async signal / config push"},{"from":"Data Ingestion Layer","to":"Feature Pipeline","contract":"New data landing triggers feature pipeline run (S3 event or scheduler)","coupling_type":"event-driven"}],"data_flow":"1. Raw events (orders, deliveries, weather, courier load) land in S3 via Data Ingestion. 2. Feature Pipeline transforms raw data → materialized features in Redis (online) and Parquet/S3 (offline). 3. Weekly: Training Pipeline reads offline features + delivery outcomes → trains model → registers in Model Registry. 4. Deployment Controller validates candidate model metrics → promotes to active → signals Prediction Service to load new model. 5. At checkout: Checkout Service calls Prediction Service → service fetches pre-computed features from Redis → runs in-memory inference → returns prediction. 6. Monitoring observes prediction quality continuously; if degradation detected, engineer triggers rollback via Deployment Controller.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Blue/Green Model Deployment","rationale":"Two model slots (active + previous) in the Prediction Service enable instant rollback without redeployment. Critical for the rollback requirement."},{"pattern":"Feature Store (Online/Offline split)","rationale":"Decouples training-time feature access (batch, large) from serving-time access (point lookup, <10ms). Ensures training-serving skew is minimized because both paths use the same feature definitions."},{"pattern":"Pre-computed Features","rationale":"Weather, courier load, and zone statistics are pre-computed and cached in Redis rather than computed at request time. This is essential to meet the 100ms P99 budget — inference alone should take <20ms, leaving no room for expensive real-time feature computation."},{"pattern":"Model Registry with Immutable Artifacts","rationale":"Every trained model is versioned and immutable. Enables auditability, reproducibility, and safe rollback."},{"pattern":"Strangler/Shadow Evaluation","rationale":"New candidate models can be shadow-scored against production traffic before promotion, catching regressions before they affect users."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Prediction Serving","classification":"core","aggregates":["PredictionRequest","ModelSlot (active/previous)","FeatureVector"]},{"name":"Feature Engineering","classification":"core","aggregates":["FeatureDefinition","MaterializedFeature","FeatureEntity (zone, courier_region, time_bucket)"]},{"name":"Model Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":["TrainedModel (artifact + metrics + version)","TrainingRun","EvaluationResult"]},{"name":"Data Collection","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["DeliveryEvent","WeatherSnapshot","CourierLoadSnapshot"]},{"name":"Observability","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PredictionLog","LatencyMetric","DriftAlert"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Feature Engineering","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Open Host Service — online feature store exposes a stable key-value interface consumed by prediction service"},{"upstream":"Feature Engineering","downstream":"Model Lifecycle","relationship":"Conformist — training pipeline consumes features as-is from the offline store"},{"upstream":"Model Lifecycle","downstream":"Prediction Serving","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — prediction service consumes model artifacts; deployment controller mediates promotion"},{"upstream":"Data Collection","downstream":"Feature Engineering","relationship":"Published Language — raw events in a known schema land in S3; feature pipeline transforms them"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Pre-computed features in Redis vs real-time feature computation","gains":"Guarantees <10ms feature fetch, makes P99 <100ms achievable, simplifies prediction service logic","sacrifices":"Features may be slightly stale (staleness bounded by materialization frequency — minutes for courier load, hourly for weather). Adds operational overhead of Redis.","alternatives_considered":["Real-time feature computation at request time — rejected because weather API calls and courier load aggregation would blow the 100ms budget","Embedded feature cache in prediction service — rejected because cache invalidation across instances is complex"]},{"decision":"Blue/green model slots vs canary/gradual rollout","gains":"Simple to implement and reason about for a 4-person team. Instant rollback is trivial — just swap pointer.","sacrifices":"No gradual traffic shifting. Model changes are all-or-nothing after shadow evaluation.","alternatives_considered":["Canary deployment with traffic splitting — adds complexity (request routing, metric comparison per variant) that a 4-person team shouldn't take on initially","A/B testing framework — overkill for weekly retraining; can add later if needed"]},{"decision":"Manual rollback vs automated rollback","gains":"Prevents false-positive auto-rollbacks from triggering during transient metric fluctuations. Keeps human in the loop for a 4-person team where context is high.","sacrifices":"Rollback latency is bounded by human response time (minutes, not seconds).","alternatives_considered":["Automated rollback on metric threshold breach — risky with small team; alert fatigue and false positives can cause model version thrashing"]},{"decision":"Single model (e.g., LightGBM/XGBoost) vs deep learning","gains":"Tabular features with 6 inputs are the sweet spot for gradient-boosted trees. Sub-5ms inference, tiny artifact size, interpretable, fast training. Fits the latency budget easily.","sacrifices":"Potentially less accurate for complex non-linear interactions — but with 6 features, this is unlikely to matter.","alternatives_considered":["Neural network — slower inference, harder to debug, overkill for tabular data with few features","Linear regression — too simple, won't capture zone/weather/load interactions well"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Model algorithm","evolution_path":"The prediction service loads a generic model artifact (e.g., ONNX or framework-native). Swapping from XGBoost to a neural net requires only a new training pipeline step and compatible artifact format — no serving changes."},{"area":"Feature additions","evolution_path":"Adding new features (e.g., traffic data, holiday flag) requires: (1) new feature pipeline job, (2) new Redis key materialization, (3) updated feature vector schema. Prediction service auto-consumes whatever features the model expects."},{"area":"Canary/A/B deployment","evolution_path":"Blue/green slots can evolve into N model slots with traffic-splitting logic in the Prediction Service when the team grows or experimentation becomes important."},{"area":"Real-time features","evolution_path":"If staleness becomes a problem, individual features can be migrated from batch-computed to stream-computed (e.g., Kafka Streams/Flink for courier load) without changing the online store interface."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"P99 latency budget","reason":"100ms at P99 is a hard constraint from checkout UX. This constrains model complexity, feature computation strategy, and infrastructure choices. Cannot be relaxed without business approval."},{"area":"Feature store as the single source of truth for features","reason":"Training and serving MUST use the same feature definitions to avoid training-serving skew. This contract is non-negotiable — skew is the #1 silent killer in ML systems."},{"area":"Model Registry immutability","reason":"Once registered, a model artifact and its metadata are immutable. This is essential for reproducibility and safe rollback. Mutations here would undermine the entire versioning scheme."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Training-serving skew: features computed differently at training time vs serving time, causing silent prediction degradation","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Single feature definition codebase used by both offline and online materialization paths. Integration tests that compare feature values from both paths on the same input."},{"risk":"Stale features in Redis causing degraded predictions (e.g., courier load from 30 min ago)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Feature freshness monitoring with alerts. TTLs on Redis keys so stale features fail loudly rather than silently serving old data. Fallback to default values with a degraded prediction flag."},{"risk":"Model regression not caught before promotion","severity":"high","mitigation":"Automated offline evaluation gates in the training pipeline (must beat current model on holdout set). Optional shadow scoring on live traffic before promotion. Deployment Controller blocks promotion if gates fail."},{"risk":"Redis failure causing prediction service outage during checkout","severity":"high","mitigation":"Redis deployed as a cluster with replicas. Prediction service has a fallback path: if feature fetch fails, use a simpler heuristic model (e.g., zone-average lookup table) and return a degraded prediction rather than failing checkout."},{"risk":"4-person team spread too thin across ML + platform concerns","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use managed services where possible (e.g., managed Redis, S3, managed orchestration like Airflow/Step Functions). Avoid building custom infra. The 2 ML engineers own feature pipeline + training pipeline; the 2 platform engineers own prediction service + deployment controller + monitoring."},{"risk":"Weekly retraining may not capture rapid distribution shifts (e.g., sudden weather event, holiday surge)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Monitor prediction error rate in real-time. If error spikes, trigger an ad-hoc retraining run. Design the training pipeline to be triggerable on-demand, not only on schedule."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (weeks 1-3): Build the prediction service with a hand-tuned heuristic model (distance/zone lookup table) to establish the serving infrastructure and meet the latency SLA. Deploy Redis for online features with courier load and weather. Phase 2 (weeks 4-6): Build the offline feature pipeline and training pipeline. Train initial XGBoost model, register in Model Registry, deploy via blue/green swap. Phase 3 (weeks 7-8): Add monitoring, alerting, shadow evaluation, and rollback automation. Phase 4 (ongoing): Iterate on features, model complexity, and retraining frequency based on observed error rates.","files_to_create_or_modify":["services/prediction-service/ — Stateless gRPC/REST service (Python or Go). Loads model on startup, fetches features from Redis, runs inference.","services/prediction-service/models/ — Blue/green model loader with hot-swap capability.","pipelines/feature-pipeline/ — Airflow DAGs or Step Functions for batch feature materialization.","pipelines/training-pipeline/ — Weekly training job: data assembly → train → evaluate → register.","infra/model-registry/ — S3 bucket + DynamoDB/Postgres metadata table for model versions.","infra/feature-store/ — Redis cluster config + TTL policies + materialization scripts.","tools/deployment-controller/ — CLI/API for model promotion and rollback.","monitoring/dashboards/ — Grafana dashboards for latency, prediction distribution, error rate, feature freshness.","tests/integration/feature-skew-test — Validates offline and online feature parity."],"patterns_to_follow":"Use XGBoost or LightGBM for the initial model — fast inference, excellent on tabular data. Serve the model in-process (no separate model server) to avoid network hop latency. Use ONNX Runtime if you want framework-agnostic serving. Keep the prediction service stateless — model is loaded from registry on startup/signal, features are fetched from Redis. Use structured logging with model_version in every prediction for debugging.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["DO NOT compute features at request time — pre-compute everything. Even a single external API call (weather) in the hot path will blow your latency budget.","DO NOT use a separate model serving framework (TF Serving, Triton) for a single XGBoost model — it adds network latency and operational complexity for no benefit at this scale.","DO NOT skip the feature skew test — it is the single most important test in an ML system.","DO NOT auto-rollback without human confirmation at this team size — the risk of thrashing outweighs the speed benefit.","DO NOT store model artifacts in the database — use S3 with metadata references. Models can be hundreds of MBs.","DO NOT neglect the fallback path — checkout cannot fail because the ML system is down. Always have a degraded-but-functional heuristic fallback."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Latency architecture — pre-computed features + in-process inference is the proven pattern for <100ms ML serving","Blue/green model deployment for rollback — simple, battle-tested, right-sized for a 4-person team","XGBoost/LightGBM as the initial model choice for tabular data with few features","Feature store split (online/offline) to prevent training-serving skew"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact Redis sizing and cluster topology — depends on feature cardinality and traffic volume (not specified)","Whether weekly retraining is sufficient — depends on how volatile delivery patterns are in practice","Team workload distribution — 4 people across this scope is tight; some phases may need to be serialized"],"assumptions":["Traffic volume is moderate (thousands of predictions per minute, not millions — otherwise we'd need to discuss sharding and geo-distribution)","The checkout service is the only consumer of predictions (no batch prediction use case)","Weather data is available via a reliable external API with reasonable rate limits","The team has access to AWS or equivalent cloud managed services (S3, Redis, orchestration)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 <100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot path (feature lookup + model inference) from the cold path (training pipeline + model registry) to independently optimize latency and throughput. A lightweight feature store bridges both paths. Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a blue/green model deployment pattern behind a routing layer. Designed for a 4-perso
